practices: The course framework is structured around four AP Economics Skills. Skill Category 1, Principles and Models (30-42% of MCQs), involves defining, describing, and comparing economic principles and models. Skill Category 2, Interpretation (37-47% of MCQs), requires students to explain given economic outcomes. Skill Category 3, Manipulation (16-25% of MCQs), focuses on determining the outcomes of specific economic situations. Skill Category 4, Graphing and Visuals (30-50% of FRQ points), assesses the ability to model economic situations using graphs and visual representations. Numerical analysis, involving calculations and quantitative interpretation, is integrated across the first three skill categories and comprises 20-30% of the multiple-choice questions.

keyTopics: Scarcity and its causes
Resource allocation and economic systems
The Production Possibilities Curve (PPC) model
Comparative advantage, absolute advantage, and gains from trade
Cost-benefit analysis and opportunity cost
Marginal analysis and consumer choice theory
Diminishing marginal utility
Law of demand and determinants of demand
Law of supply and determinants of supply
Price elasticity of demand and supply
Income elasticity and cross-price elasticity of demand
Market equilibrium, consumer surplus, and producer surplus
Effects of government intervention: price ceilings, price floors, taxes, and subsidies
International trade, tariffs, and public policy

units.unitTitle: Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model
keyTopics: The production function: marginal product and diminishing returns
Short-run vs. long-run costs (fixed, variable, total, average, marginal)
Economies and diseconomies of scale
Profit: accounting vs. economic profit
Profit maximization rule: MR=MC
Short-run decisions (shutdown rule) and long-run decisions (entry/exit)
Characteristics of perfectly competitive markets
Short-run and long-run equilibrium in perfect competition
Characteristics of imperfectly competitive markets and barriers to entry
Monopoly: profit maximization and inefficiency
Price discrimination
Monopolistic competition in the short run and long run (excess capacity)
Oligopoly and interdependence
Game theory: payoff matrix, dominant strategies, and Nash equilibrium
Derived demand for factors of production
Marginal revenue product (MRP) and marginal resource cost (MRC)
Profit-maximizing behavior in perfectly competitive factor (labor) markets
Determinants of factor demand and supply
Characteristics of monopsonistic markets

units.unitTitle: Market Failure and the Role of Government
keyTopics: Socially efficient vs. inefficient market outcomes and deadweight loss
Positive and negative externalities
Public goods, private goods, and the free-rider problem
Government intervention to correct market failures (e.g., taxes, subsidies)
Inequality in income and wealth
The Lorenz curve and Gini coefficient (conceptual understanding)
Sources of income and wealth inequality

examFormat.freeResponse: 3 questions (1 long, 2 short), 60 minutes (includes a 10-minute reading period), 33.35% of total score

antiPatterns: The CED explicitly states several topics and tasks are beyond the scope of the course and AP Exam. These include: drawing indifference curves; drawing graphs of quotas; drawing graphs of inefficiency due to collusion; drawing the Lorenz curve; and calculating Gini coefficients. In game theory, the exam does not cover scenarios with more than two players or more than two actions per player, mixed-strategy equilibria, or extensive form games. Students are expected to understand the concepts behind these excluded items (e.g., how quotas affect quantity, the concept of the Lorenz curve) but will not be required to perform the graphical or complex calculative tasks on the exam.
